phishing.definition: Phishing is a cyberattack where attackers disguise themselves as trusted entities to trick victims into revealing sensitive information like passwords, credit card numbers, or personal data.
types: Spear Phishing: Targeted attacks on specific individuals using personal information
Whaling: Attacks targeting high-profile executives or VIPs
Smishing: Phishing via SMS text messages
Vishing: Voice phishing via phone calls
Clone Phishing: Duplicating legitimate emails with malicious links
indicators: Urgent or threatening language ('Your account will be suspended!')
Mismatched or suspicious sender email domains
Generic greetings like 'Dear Customer' instead of your name
Requests for sensitive information via email
Suspicious URLs that mimic legitimate sites (paypa1.com instead of paypal.com)
examples: Fake bank email claiming suspicious activity requiring immediate login
Fake IT department email asking you to reset your password via a link
Fake package delivery notification with malicious tracking link
Fake prize/lottery winning notification requiring personal details
prevention: Always verify sender email addresses carefully
Hover over links before clicking to see the real URL
Go directly to websites by typing the URL instead of clicking links
Enable multi-factor authentication (MFA) on all accounts
Use email filtering and anti-phishing tools
When in doubt, call the organization directly to verify

malware.definition: Malware (malicious software) is any software intentionally designed to cause disruption, damage, or unauthorized access to computer systems.
types: Viruses: Self-replicating code that attaches to legitimate programs
Trojans: Malware disguised as legitimate software
Spyware: Secretly monitors user activity and collects data
Adware: Displays unwanted advertisements, often bundled with free software
Rootkits: Hides malware presence from the operating system
Keyloggers: Records keystrokes to steal passwords and data
Worms: Self-replicating malware that spreads across networks without user action
Fileless Malware: Operates in memory without leaving files on disk
prevention: Install reputable antivirus/anti-malware software
Keep all software and operating systems updated
Avoid downloading software from untrusted sources
Don't open unexpected email attachments
Use a firewall
Regularly scan your system
Be cautious with USB drives from unknown sources

ransomware.definition: Ransomware is a type of malware that encrypts the victim's files and demands payment (usually cryptocurrency) for the decryption key.
how_it_works: 1. Initial infection via phishing email, malicious download, or RDP exploit
2. Malware establishes persistence on the system
7. May exfiltrate data before encryption (double extortion)
famous_attacks: WannaCry (2017): Infected 200,000+ computers in 150 countries, caused $4B+ in damages
NotPetya (2017): Disguised as ransomware, actually a wiper; caused $10B+ in damages
Colonial Pipeline (2021): Shut down major US fuel pipeline, $4.4M ransom paid
REvil/Kaseya (2021): Affected 1,500+ businesses through MSP supply chain attack
prevention: Maintain regular, tested backups (3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite)
Keep systems fully patched and updated
Use email filtering to block malicious attachments
Restrict user privileges (principle of least privilege)
Disable Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) if not needed
Implement network segmentation
Train employees on phishing awareness
if_infected: DO NOT pay the ransom — payment doesn't guarantee recovery
Immediately isolate infected systems from the network
Report to FBI (IC3.gov) or relevant authorities
Engage a professional incident response team

social_engineering.definition: Social engineering manipulates people psychologically to divulge confidential information or perform actions that compromise security.
techniques: Pretexting: Creating a fabricated scenario to extract information
Baiting: Offering something enticing (free USB drive) with hidden malware
Quid Pro Quo: Offering services in exchange for information
Tailgating/Piggybacking: Physically following authorized personnel into restricted areas
Impersonation: Pretending to be IT support, executives, or vendors
psychological_triggers: Authority: Impersonating bosses, police, or government officials
Urgency: 'Act now or lose your account!'
Social Proof: 'Everyone else has already done this'
Reciprocity: Doing something for you first to create obligation
Fear: Threats of consequences if you don't comply

man_in_the_middle.definition: MITM attacks occur when an attacker secretly intercepts and potentially alters communications between two parties who believe they are directly communicating.
types: SSL Stripping: Downgrading HTTPS to HTTP
Wi-Fi Eavesdropping: Setting up rogue hotspots
ARP Spoofing: Linking attacker's MAC to legitimate IP
DNS Spoofing: Redirecting DNS queries to malicious sites
Email Hijacking: Intercepting email communications
prevention: Always use HTTPS websites (look for padlock icon)
Avoid public Wi-Fi for sensitive transactions
Use a VPN on public networks
Enable certificate pinning in applications
Use end-to-end encrypted communications

sql_injection.definition: SQL Injection is an attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into database queries, potentially allowing attackers to view, modify, or delete database data.
sql_injection.example: Entering ' OR '1'='1 in a login field to bypass authentication
prevention: Use parameterized queries/prepared statements
Implement input validation and sanitization
Use a Web Application Firewall (WAF)
Apply least privilege to database accounts
Regularly audit database activity

ddos.definition: A Distributed Denial of Service attack floods a target with traffic from multiple sources to overwhelm and disrupt services.
types: Volumetric Attacks: Flooding bandwidth with junk traffic
Protocol Attacks: Exploiting network protocol weaknesses
Application Layer Attacks: Targeting web servers and applications
prevention: Use DDoS protection services (Cloudflare, AWS Shield)
Implement rate limiting
Use Content Delivery Networks (CDNs)
Have an incident response plan ready
Monitor traffic for anomalies

rules: Mix uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols
Change passwords immediately if breach suspected
strong_examples: Tr0ub4dor&3 (but don't use this now it's famous!)

mfa.definition: Multi-Factor Authentication requires two or more verification methods to access an account.
types: Something you know: Password, PIN
Something you have: Phone (authenticator app, SMS), hardware token
Something you are: Fingerprint, face recognition
best_options: Authenticator apps (Google Authenticator, Authy) — most secure
SMS codes — better than nothing but susceptible to SIM swapping
Email codes — weakest form of MFA

software_updates: Enable automatic updates for OS and software
Update firmware on routers and IoT devices
Patch management is one of the most critical security controls
60% of breaches involve known vulnerabilities that had patches available
network_security: Create a separate guest network for IoT devices
Consider a VPN for sensitive browsing

details: 1 copy stored offsite (cloud or physical location)
Test your backups regularly — untested backups are not backups

email_security: Verify senders before clicking links or attachments
Use email encryption for sensitive communications (PGP/S/MIME)
Check for email authentication (SPF, DKIM, DMARC)
browsing_safety: Only visit HTTPS sites for sensitive transactions
Use a reputable browser with security features
Use Privacy Badger or similar tracker blockers
Consider using Tor Browser for privacy-sensitive browsing

suspicious_indicators: URL shorteners hiding the real destination
HTTP instead of HTTPS for login pages
Unusual top-level domains (.xyz, .tk, .ml for banking sites)
IP addresses instead of domain names
Excessively long URLs with random characters
URLs with @ symbol (everything before @ is ignored)
Mismatched anchor text and actual URL
how_to_check: Hover over the link to see the actual URL in the status bar
Use VirusTotal.com to scan suspicious URLs
Use URLVoid.com or URLScan.io for URL reputation checks
Check WHOIS data for domain registration date (very new = suspicious)

steps: 1. IDENTIFY: Recognize and confirm the security incident
2. CONTAIN: Isolate affected systems to prevent spread
3. ERADICATE: Remove the threat (malware, unauthorized access)
4. RECOVER: Restore systems from clean backups
5. DOCUMENT: Record everything for analysis and legal purposes
6. REPORT: Notify relevant parties (IT, management, authorities if needed)
7. LEARN: Conduct post-incident review to prevent recurrence
contacts: FBI Internet Crime Complaint Center: www.ic3.gov

quick_tips: 🔐 Use a unique password for every account
📱 Enable 2FA on all important accounts today
💾 Back up your data with the 3-2-1 rule
🎣 Verify before you click any link
📶 Use a VPN on public Wi-Fi
🔍 Monitor your accounts for suspicious activity
